title: No-go theorems on simulating uncertainty principle's signatures
authors: Chung-Yun Hsieh, Minjeong Song, Shin-Liang Chen
abstract: Uncertainty principle, one of the most iconic features of quantum mechanics, was originally viewed as a fundamental limitation. Since the inception of quantum information science, researchers began to use it to achieve quantum advantages. To better understand the origin of these advantages, an essential question is: To what extent can the uncertainty principle's signatures be simulated by a single measurement? As a single measurement clearly cannot demonstrate the uncertainty principle, such a simulation, if exists, implies the claimed advantages may either stem from other quantum features, or just be reproducible in a less resourceful way. In this work, we report a series of noise-robust no-go theorems, showing that strong enough signatures of uncertainty principle cannot be simulated by a single measurement, even when assisted by quantum pre- or post-processing. This signature is modelled by complementary instruments. We completely characterise complementary instruments by a numerically feasible measure and show that they are necessary and sufficient resources for the advantage in an operational task that aims to unambiguously send classical information.
summary: The paper rigorously analyzes the uncertainty principle by introducing 'complementarity' as a concept stronger than simple incompatibility for quantum measurements. It proves that strong signatures of this principle cannot be simulated by single-measurement processes, thereby establishing fundamental no-go theorems regarding resource simulation in quantum information theory.
main_problem: The core problem is determining the fundamental limits on simulating signatures of the uncertainty principle using only a single quantum measurement process (instrument). The authors aim to establish a hierarchy between different notions of quantum compatibility.
main_result: They prove that strong signatures of the uncertainty principle cannot be simulated by a single measurement, even when assisted by quantum pre- or post-processing. They introduce 'complementarity' as a resource strictly stronger than mere incompatibility.
method: The work employs rigorous mathematical characterizations using Semidefinite Programming (SDP) to define and test concepts like complementarity and joint measurability for generalized quantum instruments. Operational tests involve analyzing the error probability in unambiguously sending classical information.
model_or_system: The study models general measurement processes using 'instruments' $\{E_a\}_{a}$, which are generalizations of POVMs, applied to bipartite quantum systems often involving maximally entangled states $|\Phi^+angle$. The analysis extends to noisy versions of these instruments.
key_observables: Complementary overlap $C(a,b)(E)$, error probability in unambiguous classical communication ($P(	ilde{\eta})_{	ext{error}}$), and the joint measurability ($\mathcal{J}\mathcal{M}$) condition.
important_parameters: The noise parameter $\epsilon$ (for noisy Pauli instruments) and the fidelity/overlap parameters derived from SDP solutions.
main_assumptions: The analysis often focuses on rank-one projective instruments for establishing the strongest no-go theorems, and some results rely on assuming positive probabilities for input settings ($p_x > 0$).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the central question of simulating two-instrument signatures with one. Figure 2 helps define complementary instruments by showing non-vanishing overlap.
paper_structure: The paper builds logically by first defining incompatibility, then introducing the stronger concept of complementarity via vanishing overlap (characterized by SDP). It then proves that complementarity implies incompatibility and establishes operational equivalences: complementarity is equivalent to achieving unambiguous classical communication regardless of outcome relabeling. Finally, it uses these results to construct no-go theorems against simulation.
why_interesting: This work provides fundamental theoretical constraints on what quantum measurements can achieve, directly impacting the understanding of resource theories in quantum information science and setting benchmarks for quantum advantage claims.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Main question. When can uncertainty principle’s signa- tures, as carried by a pair of instruments, be simulated by a single instrument? In the figure, the left box is an instrument with classical outcome 𝜆, and the right box implements the 𝑥-th instrument (with classical outcomes 𝑎) when we press the 𝑥-th button.

FIG. 2. Non-vanishing overlap. Equation (2) implies that (E𝑎|0 ⊗I𝐴)(𝜌) ≥(L ⊗I𝐴)(𝜌) and (E𝑏|1 ⊗I𝐴)(𝜌) ≥(L ⊗I𝐴)(𝜌) for every finite-dimensional auxiliary system 𝐴and joint state 𝜌 (here, I𝐴is the identity channel acting on 𝐴). Hence, both the classi- cal outcomes and quantum outputs of E𝑎|0 and E𝑏|1 can be estimated by applying a single instrument containing L as an element [50].
